Why mass applications don't work and how to do it right

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Tessa Whelan
10mo
  • Report this post

Submitting mass applications is not an effective strategy to get hired. Spraying out dozens (or hundreds) of resumes and hoping something sticks often leads to frustration, not results. Instead, be intentional: Research the companies that align with your values and goals. Tailor your resume and outreach to highlight why you’re a strong fit. Build relationships—connect with people on the inside and show genuine interest. Think quality over quantity. Strategic, focused applications take more effort up front—but they give you a much better chance of landing interviews that actually move you toward the career you want. 💡 Job searching is about showing why you belong in the role and company, not just that you’re available for hire.

223 38 Comments
Like Comment
Kleril Odd 10mo
  • Report this comment

Do you have guidance for how to build genuine insider connections? There's a pretty even split from what I hear from folks in Talent Acquisition about how kosher they find any form of 'reaching out'. Obviously there's degrees of tact that can be employed in attempts at outreach, but I see a ton of general scorn thrown at applicants for trying to build that human connection before reaching the interview stage - many see it as an attempt to circumvent 'The Process' and jump the line to have *any* contact with potential applicants before being across the table. I've definitely been left hesitant to reach out as a result, I'm terrified of burning a bridge before I've even sent in an application.

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
Nathan Daly 9mo
  • Report this comment

The only job applications worth researching and putting time and effort into are those where you have a connection at the company. The reason people spray and pray job applications is because it does work. All most people are trying to do is get an interview. If you do a custom tailored, resume, cover letter, LinkedIn outreach and an intro video for example that application is going to take well over an hour. Most companies will just send you an automated rejection email when their AI resume scanner doesn't think you have the experience and you just wasted your time. You've been at CoLab almost 4 years, that's to say it's been a while since you've HAD to apply for a job while you're unemployed. The job market and applying for jobs isn't what it used to be I've done both the extremely detailed and well researched application and the spray and pray. I definitely got more responses from thoughtful applications but it was still probably only a 10-15% success rate to get an interview, vs mass applying I might get a 4-5% interview rate but I could apply to way more jobs. Knowing someone at the company I've had a 100% success rate getting an interview, so it is what it is.

Like
Reply
2 Reactions 3 Reactions
David Owens 10mo
  • Report this comment

Here is the problem. In an environment in which some 20 - 70 % of all job postings are fake and are never intended to be filled, being intentional is also a zero sum game. You spent a lot of time customizing an application for something that will never yield a result no matter what you do. This is a new environment and the old conventional wisdom just does not apply. Everyone needs to try new techniques and think outside of the box. In software development with the number of fake postings and the large amount of ghosting the volume play may be the only play in town. We also may need to understand that not only are we competing against all of the other unemployed software developers out there, we are also competing against AI. We will never be able to compete against AI if we do not jettison the usual 2 page resume, list only skills you have, and make yourself an easy to search for commodity. We will never out commodity a commodity like AI no matter how hard we try. It may not make recruiters jobs easier, but if AI wins recruiters won't have jobs either - so do them a favor. Make your resume reflect an authentic human being, separate yourselves from AI.

Like
Reply
3 Reactions 4 Reactions
Yuri M. 10mo
  • Report this comment

I believe tailoring a resume is important, but it can sometimes lead candidates to overstate or misrepresent their experiences. For me, the key is striking a balance — highlight what’s most relevant without creating inaccuracies

More Relevant Posts

  • Susana Onyowoicho O.
    9mo
    • Report this post

    I hacked my way into 12 job interviews in 30 days without applying through a single job portal. Just ONE strategy most people are too scared to try. I call it: The CEO Cold Email. And before you say that's networking, no. This is different. Here's the game: Most people apply for jobs the normal way: → See job posting → Click apply → Submit resume → Get lost in a pile of 200 other resumes I do the OPPOSITE. Step 1: Find the CEO (or founder) Not HR. Not the hiring manager. The DECISION-MAKER. → Go to the company website → Find their "About" or "Team" page → Get the CEO's name → Find their email using Hunter.io (free tool) or Contact Out Step 2: Write an email that doesn't sound like BEGGING Most people write: "Dear Sir/Madam, I am writing to express my interest..." SNOOZE. Here's what I write: Subject: Quick question about [Company Name]'s growth Hi [CEO Name], I've been following [Company Name] for a while, specifically how you've scaled [mention something specific about their company: recent funding, etc]. I'm curious: are you currently building out your [department related to your skills] team?I've spent the last [X years] doing [specific result you achieved], and I have a few ideas on how [Company Name] could [solve a specific problem they likely have]. Just wondering if it's worth a conversation. [Your Name] [Link to your LinkedIn] Step 3: The follow-up (this is where most people fail) If they don't respond in 3 days, send this: Hi [CEO Name], I know you're busy (running a company and all 😅). Just wanted to bump this in case it got buried. Still happy to share a few ideas on [specific problem] if you're open to it. [Your Name] Step 4: The proof follow-up If they STILL don't respond, send this 5 days later: Subject: A Document to scale your Company Hi [CEO Name], I know I've been in your inbox a couple times (sorry!). But I couldn't help myself, I put together a quick doc on [specific idea for their company]. Just thought it might be useful.[Link to Google Doc with your ideas] Hope it helps! [Your Name] What happened when I tried this: → Sent 40 emails → 12 responded → 7 turned into calls → 3 turned into job offers The ones who didn't respond? I applied through their normal channels with a line in my cover letter: "I reached out to [CEO Name] directly with ideas on [problem]. I'd love to discuss further." Got 4 MORE interviews that way. The psychology behind this: CEOs respect INITIATIVE. When everyone else is applying through the portal, you go straight to the top. That signals: → Confidence → Resourcefulness → You're not afraid to break the rules Those are the people companies want. Will this work for every industry? No. Big corporations? Probably not. Startups? Scaleups? Small businesses?GOLD MINE. Stop waiting for job postings to save you. Start CREATING opportunities. P.S.Who's brave enough to try this? PPS.What can you add to these to make it Irresistible?

    292 357 Comments
    Like Comment

  • Angela Lau
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Recruiters are just an obstacle in your job search. NOT. They are your biggest asset. So instead of treating recruiter calls like a formality. Treat them like strategy sessions. Here are 7 smart questions to ask a recruiter so you walk into interviews fully prepared and in control: 1/ What is the top challenge this role is solving? ↳ Understanding the pain point helps you tailor your pitch. 2/ What does the hiring manager value most in a candidate? ↳ Skills on paper are one thing, but knowing what actually matters is another. 3/ What’s the company’s biggest priority right now? ↳ Aligning your answers with company goals makes you stand out. 4/ What is the focus of [X round] of the interview? ↳ Knowing what they’re evaluating helps you prepare the right examples. 5/ What’s the interview process like? ↳ Knowing what to expect allows you to prepare effectively. 6/ Are there any unique interests or work styles the hiring manager has? ↳ Small details help you build rapport and make a lasting impression. 7/ What’s the salary range for this role? ↳ Get clarity upfront so you can negotiate from a position of power. Most candidates go into interviews hoping to impress. The best candidates go in knowing exactly how to win. Recruiters have insider knowledge. Ask for help. ♻️ Repost to help a friend with a recruiter screen this week. 🔔 Follow Angela Lau for more.

    274 48 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Balaji Kummari
    8mo
    • Report this post

    There's a way to turn your application into an interview 4 times faster. Most people never use it because they think their network isn't big enough. The candidates who land roles in 6-8 weeks aren't sending more applications. They're activating connections they already have. Former coworkers. Professional Slack groups. Your college alumni database. People in your open-source community. These aren't strangers. They're one conversation away from turning you into an internal referral. I'm going to show you how to convert these connections into referrals without feeling pushy or transactional: Most people message someone once and immediately ask for a referral. That's why it doesn't work. You need three intentional touches over two weeks. Start with curiosity. Ask someone at your target company what surprised them in their first 90 days on the team. People love sharing what they learned. No ask. Just real interest. Four days later, share something you built that solves a problem their team faces. A quick Loom video. A GitHub repo. A writeup with one clear result. This shows you can actually do the work. Ten to fourteen days in, reference a specific job posting and attach your tailored resume. Make it effortless for them to refer you by writing a one-paragraph summary they can copy into their company's referral form. Track what converts. How many lead to screens. How many conversations turn into internal forwards. Adjust your approach based on real feedback. Cold applications are a lottery. Warm referrals are a strategy. Which network have you been sitting on that could cut your search time in half?

    4
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Saad Bin Shafiq
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Last week, I got a message from someone who had just been rejected for a role he really wanted. He wrote: “Wow Saad! 2 years ago, recruiters were reaching out to me every week. Now I can’t even get interviews. I send 50 applications a week and hear nothing.” He sounded discouraged and defeated, so I called him. As we spoke, I couldn’t stop thinking about how many talented people must be feeling the exact same way right now - questioning their worth, replaying past interviews, trying to figure out what invisible line they crossed that suddenly made them “unhirable.” If that’s you, let me say this - founder to job seeker, recruiter to candidate, human to human: It is NOT you. Here’s what I see from my side as someone whose company has now processed over 2 million job applications across Fortune 500 clients. Since 2021, hiring has completely shifted. Back then, recruiters couldn’t hire fast enough. Candidates with solid experience and half-decent resumes were getting inbound messages every week. Today? The same companies that were adding 200 people a year are now debating whether to open a single role. But the biggest change isn’t just economic, it’s technical. Most job seekers don’t realize this, but a large portion of the hiring funnel is being filtered by outdated systems built decades ago ie KEYWORD MATCHING aka ATS That means if your resume says “JS” instead of “JavaScript,” you might never even reach a recruiter’s inbox. 70% of resumes are rejected before a human ever sees them. Not because candidates aren’t qualified but because the system was built to scan words, not understand people. I know what that feels like. Before I built Nodes, I applied to 700 jobs and got rejected 699 times. It wasn’t because I wasn’t capable. It was because I didn’t fit the system’s pattern. That experience still drives me today and hence became the reason why I built this company. So if you’re out there, sending applications into the void, wondering if it’s even worth it…. Keep going. Keep showing up. Keep believing in your craft, your experience and your story. Because the market will shift again, it always does. And when it does, the people who kept moving forward will be the ones most ready to seize it. Saad ❤️

    17
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Todd Linder
    8mo
    • Report this post

    These 4 things are killing your job search. (save this) Plus 1 hack at the end to get more interviews. 1️⃣ Doom scrolling on LinkedIn.   Through the feed and the job boards.   95%+ of what comes across your screen will not be relevant to you. It will distract you. And will only make you feel worse.   2️⃣ Applying to everything that looks good.   Getting a job is not like a buffet. You can’t just select what you want and hope it tastes good. It takes work to be a great candidate that stands out to hiring managers.   3️⃣ Sending generic resumes.   Hiring managers are looking for a solution to their problem. Your job is to prove that you are the solution. And the best one out of all of the other solutions. That means customizing your resume to the job description every time.   4️⃣ Applying without following up.   A friend recently posted that they had 500+ applicants for a job. Only 1 person reached out personally 🤯 It’s not that hard to stand out by going the extra mile (or foot in this case).     A little hack?   Send a Loom video to introduce yourself with your application. Everyone likes watching video. And now you’re not just a bunch of words on a page, but a person.   -- I go deeper on job search strategies with 748+ subscribers in our newsletter each week.   Click "View my newsletter" to get the Ministry to Marketplace Minute in your inbox every Friday.

  • Sole Hire

    2,070 followers

    8mo
    • Report this post

    🚀 You hit “submit” on that application… now what? Here are 4 smart moves to stand out after applying: 1️⃣ Reach out to the Hiring Manager on LinkedIn – a quick message puts your name on their radar. 2️⃣ Tailor your resume to the role – small keyword tweaks can make a big impact. 3️⃣ Answer your phone professionally – “Hi, this is [Your Name]” goes a long way. 4️⃣ Be ready for a quick interview – research the company right away, calls can come fast. These steps show initiative, professionalism, and preparation — the exact traits hiring managers look for. 📞 (305) 351-1087 📧 recruitment@solehire.com 🔗solehire.com

  • Emma Cambon
    8mo
    • Report this post

    👉 If you want more interviews, here’s one truth to hold on to: Recruiters are busy. They spend seconds on your resume and silently ask: “Is this person worth interviewing?” Your job? Make that answer an easy yes. Now picture this: you’re watching Harry Potter. The plot kicks in… but none of the characters have backstories, or personalities. 🧙♂️ That’s what a recruiter feels when they open a resume with no story, no focus, and nothing memorable. They close it. And forget. ➡️ 𝐇𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐢𝐬 𝐚 𝐜𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝐫𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐩𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐥𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐯𝐢𝐞𝐰𝐬 𝐒𝐭𝐞𝐩 𝟏: 𝐔𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝟑𝟎-𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐝 𝐑𝐮𝐥𝐞 Recruiters take 30 seconds to decide if you’re worth interviewing. Give them clarity. Let your resume speak for you in seconds. Quality > Quantity Let them spot your fit and uniqueness in seconds. Don’t make them scroll through a wall of text that feels like a dictionary page. 𝐒𝐭𝐞𝐩 𝟐: 𝐀𝐝𝐝 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐝𝐢𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬 Most resumes stop at: “Job Title with X years of experience doing task A, B, and C.” It’s not wrong. It’s just what everyone else does. But if you want to stand out, show recruiters something deeper: real alignment. Go beyond job scope. Here’s what that means, and how to do it: Checklist: a.     Industry overlap → (Tech, Pharma, Education…) b.    Work context → (Remote teams, B2B, client-facing…) c.     Company type → (Startup, SaaS, NGO, government…) d.    If you’re switching fields → clearly show your career direction (e.g. “Currently pivoting into the tech industry”) Taking a few minutes to show this kind of fit can make the recruiter stop and think: “This person gets it.” 𝐒𝐭𝐞𝐩 𝟑: 𝐌𝐢𝐱 𝐢𝐧 𝐲𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐚𝐥 𝐬𝐚𝐮𝐜𝐞 This is where most resumes go flat and feel super impersonal and impossible to remember. Here’s how to fix that. Checklist: a. Add memorable facts → Did you work at a big brand? Are you switching from law to tech? Are you a student? b. Add impact → Add metrics, KPIs, and outcomes. Numbers build trust. c. Add personal facts → Are you a parent? A marathoner? A violinist? Add one human detail. d. Remove doubt before it forms (this one is gold) → Recruiters are trained to spot red flags fast. If something looks confusing, recruiters will fill in the blanks themselves — and not always in your favor. Ask yourself: does my resume raise any questions? A short contract A long gap A sudden career change A location mismatch… Be proactive. Add context to tackle doubts as much as possible. It shows self-awareness, honesty, and confidence: 3 things recruiters instantly trust. 𝐒𝐭𝐞𝐩 𝟒: 𝐋𝐞𝐭 𝐢𝐭 𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐭 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐚𝐬𝐭𝐞 𝐚𝐠𝐚𝐢𝐧 After writing your resume, take 10 minutes. Ask yourself: will recruiters see clear alignment with this unique role? Will they remember me? Most resumes I see are safe, dry, and full of buzzwords. Yours should make someone feel something. --- 👉 Be honest: does your resume actually make you stand out?

  • Aman Kumar
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    I’ve seen 2,000+ applicants fail the simple thing regarding how they apply to a company with their resume. It’s not your skill set. It’s your submission strategy. Your perfect application is probably getting ignored. Why? Because 95% of people apply the wrong way. They treat the ATS like a lottery. The biggest secret to getting into a FAANG-level company isn’t a perfect resume. Your goal isn't just to apply. It’s to bypass the queue entirely. This is how insiders do it. Here are the 3 actions that move you from applicant to essential hire: 👇 1. The 'Context Over Connect' Rule A simple referral is weak. Contextual knowledge is power. * Action: Find 5 people currently working on your dream team. * Goal: Ask them for advice on the team’s biggest engineering challenge. Not a job. Not a referral. * Insight: They tell you the specific problems the hiring manager needs solved * You then use their exact language to tailor your resume and your story. This is how you signal, "I already understand your pain." 2. Mock Interviews: Get the Inside Track Your friends are too kind. You need constructive brutality. * Get 3+ mock interviews from people working at the exact company you’re targeting. * Reason: They know the cultural signals. They know the 3× harder technical bar. * They'll expose the micro-habits and weak points that generic interview prep misses. * You need to fail privately to execute flawlessly publicly. 3. The '60-Minute Deep Dive' Project You don't need a dozen projects. You need one project you can own completely. * Choose a single, high-complexity project that you can passionately discuss for a full hour. * The discussion must cover: trade-offs, failures, design decisions, and the 5× better outcome you achieved. * This project proves: You live this work. You didn't just code. You engineered. * The interviewer shouldn't be asking if you can code. They should be asking when you can start. They aren't hiring a resume filler. They are hiring an expert with unique context. That’s the difference between applying and getting the job. Which of these 3 steps is your bottleneck right now? 🔥

    • Report this post

    Kicking off your job search can be overwhelming, particularly if you recently got laid off or haven't looked for a job in a while. A&C Founder & President Nicole DelToro recommends a number of strategies to kick of your search, including ways to get your resume in the "interview" pile by leveraging relationships with recruiters and staying on top of what roles you've applied for. Check out her tips on our A&C blog post: https://lnkd.in/ejbfp7Mx #jobsearch #asktheexperts #accountingrecruiter #layoff #careermanagement #thursdaythoughts

  • Balaji Kummari
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Betting your entire job search on one type of company is the fastest way to waste three months. I've watched 40+ candidates cut their search time in half by doing the opposite. Most people pick BigCo or startup, go all in, and wonder why nothing moves. When the application to interview rate is 8-12%, one track isn't enough surface area; especially since the odds are that tight. I'm going to show you how to run two job application tracks at once without doubling your workload or losing your mind. Ten big company applications per week. Ten startup applications per week. Different timelines, different hiring speeds, different risk profiles. You're not putting all your chips on one bet. Here's the key: You need two versions of your resume. Same core facts. Different emphasis. The BigCo version highlights scale and process. The startup version highlights speed and ownership. They evaluate talent differently. Your resume should reflect that. The move that actually changes your odds: Pair every application with warm outreach within 48 hours. Someone who works there. A referral. A former classmate. Studies show referrals remain the highest-yield channel, yet most people skip this step entirely. Track what moves. If a company goes silent for two weeks with no interview scheduled, cut it. Redirect that energy to companies showing real hiring signals. Running both tracks gives you options. Options give you leverage. Leverage gets you better offers. Which track have you been ignoring because you thought you had to choose? (Free self-intro generator in the comments. Write your warm outreach message in 60 seconds so you can focus on sending it, not stressing over it.)
